name: Hoodoo Rootwork
description: Hoodoo Rootwork is an African American folk magic tradition rooted in West and Central African spiritual practices, blended with European folklore, Native American herbalism, and Christian beliefs. Developed during the era of slavery, it served as a means of survival, resistance, and empowerment for enslaved Africans and their descendants. Hoodoo focuses on practical magic for everyday life, addressing concerns like love, money, health, protection, and justice. It emphasizes a personal connection with the spiritual world, including ancestors, spirits of the land, and the Christian God. Rootwork utilizes herbs, roots, minerals, animal parts, personal effects, and prayers to influence outcomes and achieve desired results. Unlike Voodoo, it lacks a formal priesthood or structured pantheon. Hoodoo is a decentralized, adaptable system passed down through oral tradition and personal experience, emphasizing the power of the individual to interact with the unseen world and shape their reality. It is a living tradition that continues to evolve and adapt to contemporary challenges.
subtitle: African American Folk Magic Tradition

keyMyths.title: The Origins of Rootwork
keyMyths.description: Rootwork originated from the blending of African spiritual beliefs and practices with European and Native American traditions during the time of slavery. It provided enslaved Africans with a means of maintaining their cultural identity and spiritual power.

keyMyths.title: The Power of the Root
keyMyths.description: Roots are believed to possess potent magical properties and are used in spells and rituals to influence outcomes. The specific root chosen depends on the desired effect, such as love, protection, or healing.

sources.author: Harry Middleton Hyatt
sources.description: A comprehensive multi-volume collection of interviews and accounts of Hoodoo practitioners in the early 20th century.
sources.text: A comprehensive multi-volume collection of interviews and accounts of Hoodoo practitioners in the early 20th century.
